text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to remove the omentum adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, revealing areas of gangrene and visible fluid accumulation.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to perform detorsion of the ovary, testing the correction three times. A hemorrhagic cyst is punctured with a harmonic device, allowing separation of the inner cystic layer. Two Maryland forceps are used to hold the inner cystic layer while separating the outer layer, preparing for thorough lavage to remove hemorrhagic content.

The cystic content is placed in the anterior cul-de-sac, appearing friable due to impaired blood supply from torsion. The surgeon removes the cyst using stone holding forceps or an endo bag, followed by irrigation and suction. Adhesions are visible between the omentum and the anterior abdominal wall, potentially contributing to the torsion.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to carefully separate adhesions around the ovary. The ovarian cyst is then removed, followed by thorough lavage of the area. The surgeon performs plication of the elongated ovarian ligament using 2-0 Prolene in a purse-string suture technique, taking several consecutive bites with a needle holder while a grasper provides lateral traction on the ovary.

The surgeon tightens an extracorporeal square knot to achieve plication of the ovarian ligament, using multiple knots to secure the proline suture and prevent slipping. Excess suture is cut, followed by thorough lavage to clean the surgical area.
